exemplar.title: How did the implementation of policies such as The Aviation and Transportation Security Act and the USA PATRIOT Act impact security protocols at major U.S. airports, with an emphasis on John. F Kennedy International Airport in response to the 9/11 attacks?

detailedExemplarData.title: How did the implementation of policies such as The Aviation and Transportation Security Act and the USA PATRIOT Act impact security protocols at major U.S. airports, with an emphasis on John. F Kennedy International Airport in response to the 9/11 attacks?

strengths: The topic is framed precisely with clear historical context and geographic focus, driving a coherent investigation.
The research question is explicit, narrow, and consistently anchors the essayâs structure.
The methodological roadmap (comparing JFK with other airports and examining policy debates) is clearly signposted and maintained.
weaknesses: The bibliography, while relevant, lacks key scholarly works and exhibits inconsistencies (e.g., âlbidâ, repeated entries).
Citation practice is uneven, detracting slightly from methodological rigor.
Minor formatting lapses (double title line) suggest room for polishing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place topic communication and research question firmly in the 5â6 band, with clear, appropriate framing and focus. Methodology, however, sits in the lower half of the 3â4 band due to omissions in key literature and inconsistent citation. Balancing strong focus (5â6 band) against a partially complete methodology (3â4 band) justifies awarding 5/6 for Focus and Method.

strengths: Source material (legislation texts, TSA documents, commission reports) is generally relevant and well linked to the research question.
Subject-specific terminology (e.g., CAPPS, No-Fly List, explosive trace detection) is used accurately and consistently.
Quotations and statistical data are integrated to support key narrative points.
weaknesses: Historiographical context and deeper academic debate on security vs. liberty are underdeveloped.
Some effectiveness claims lack direct scholarly evidence, relying on journalistic sources.
Application of knowledge remains largely descriptive, with limited causal explanation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Application of source material and use of terminology align with the top of the 3â4 band, demonstrating clear relevance and accurate vocabulary. Subject knowledge sits in the mid-3â4 band due to descriptive narrative and thin historiographical engagement. Averaging these yields a solid 4/6 for Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: Research draws on appropriate, directly relevant primary and secondary sources, underpinning each section of analysis.
A coherent, reasoned argument links security measures to outcomes while acknowledging policy limitations.
Conclusions about procedural impacts and civil-liberty trade-offs are supported by data and public-opinion evidence.
weaknesses: Analytical depth occasionally lapses into assertion without sufficient counter-argument or deeper linkage to legislative objectives.
Critical evaluation is unevenâsome claims would benefit from additional risk-assessment metrics.
Discussion skews one-sided on historiographical debate, limiting the scope of evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality is strong and clearly relevant (upper 4â6 band). Analysis and discussion, while reasoned, show occasional gaps in depth and critical balance (mid 4â6 band). Because all sub-strands reside within the 4â6 band, and given particularly robust research selection, the investigation merits the maximum within that bandâ6/12 for Critical Thinking.

strengths: All required EE structural elements are present: title page with word count, table of contents, headings, conclusion, and bibliography.
Clear signposting of sections (e.g., case study vs. comparative analysis) enhances navigability.
Consistent heading styles and alphabetized bibliography support reader orientation.
weaknesses: Citation formatting is inconsistent (mixed superscripts, âlbidâ), and in-text footnotes vary in style.
Redundant title/research-question block and extraneous blank pages introduce minor distractions.
Table cell widths and pagination exhibit uneven formatting, suggesting need for a final layout review.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essayâs structure fully meets the 3â4 band for Presentation, but formatting inconsistencies and redundant elements place it at the lower end of that band. Awarding 3/4 balances overall appropriateness with areas for improvement.

strandSelections.reasoning: Formatting conventionsâconsistent heading styles, in-text footnotes, and an alphabetised bibliographyâare generally respected.  Nevertheless, citation formatting is inconsistent (mixed superscripts, âlbidâ errors) and there is no visible page numbering in the excerpt, indicating that some layout elements are not fully correct.  These shortcomings place the work at the lower end of the 3-4 band.

A reasoned argument is evident: security measures strengthened safety yet raised civil-liberty concerns.  The student cites statistical decline in attacks and public-opinion polls to support evaluation, and acknowledges limitations (e.g., 2017 breach).  Critical evaluation, however, is patchy and largely one-sided on historiographical debate, so the work fits comfortablyânot exceptionallyâwithin the 4-6 band.

The student states a precise and historically framed topic â the impact of the ATSA and USA PATRIOT Act on U.S. airport security with emphasis on JFK â in both the title page and the opening paragraph.  The time frame (post-9/11) and geographic scope (major U.S. airports, especially JFK) are unambiguous, and the investigation is clearly situated in modern U.S. history.  Because the purpose and historical focus are communicated accurately and are entirely appropriate for an EE in History, the work aligns best with the 5-6 band.  Minor wording lapses (e.g., double title line) prevent a perfect 6.

The essay contains all mandatory EE structural elements: title page with word count, table of contents, introduction, body sections with headings, conclusion and bibliography.  Logical progression largely follows the table of contents.  Minor inconsistencies (double listing of some headings, uneven pagination in the excerpt) are present but do not compromise overall appropriateness, earning the higher mark of the 3-4 band.

The bibliography shows a reasonable range of primary legislation, government hearings, commission reports and reputable secondary commentary, indicating some informed selection.  Nevertheless, several important scholarly works on U.S. aviation security or constitutional debate are missing, there is repetition within the reference list, and citation practice (e.g., âlbidâ, inconsistent superscripts) is uneven.  Methodology is thus âmostly completeâ but not fully comprehensive or consistently executed, meriting the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Most of the research selected (commission reports, federal statutes, DHS statistics, scholarly articles) is clearly relevant to exploring security-protocol change.  While not exhaustive, the evidence base directly informs each section of the discussion, meeting the descriptor of âmost research is appropriate and clearly relevantâ; breadth and pertinence justify the top mark of the 4-6 band.

The essay compares four airports, links legislative clauses to procedural shifts, and weighs short- and long-term effectiveness.  Conclusions (e.g., reduction in large-scale attacks) are usually, though not always, backed by data.  Analysis sometimes drifts into assertion and lacks deeper counter-argument, so it achieves solid but not outstanding analytical depthâmiddle of the 4-6 band.

Legislation texts, TSA documents and commission reports are quoted to illustrate pre- and post-9/11 procedures, and statistical data (flight numbers, plot counts) are linked to the argument.  Source use is generally relevant to the question, though some assertions (e.g., effectiveness claims) lack direct supporting evidence and a few sources are journalistic rather than scholarly.  Overall relevance and appropriateness are evident, fitting the upper level of the 3-4 band.

The student demonstrates clear descriptive knowledge of 9/11 events, TSA creation and the two Acts, but explanation of historical causation and historiographical context is limited.  Much of the narrative remains summary; deeper engagement with academic debates on security-versus-liberty or comparative airport studies is thin.  Hence knowledge is âclearâ yet application only partially effective, aligning with mid-band 3-4 (score 3).

The research question is explicitly formulated and repeated verbatim in several sections: âHow did the implementation of policies such as the ATSA and the USA PATRIOT Act impact security protocols at major U.S. airports, with an emphasis on JFKâ¦?â  It is sufficiently narrow, invites historical analysis and comparison, and remains the guiding thread of the essay.  Although the discussion occasionally drifts into present-day commentary, overall focus is maintained, placing the strand solidly in the 5-6 band.

Terminology such as âCAPPSâ, âNo-Fly Listâ, âexplosive trace detectionâ, and references to legislative Titles are used accurately and consistently.  Occasional minor slips (âlbidâ, âinterceptâ¦ terrorism actâ) do not impede clarity.  The sustained and correct use of subject-specific vocabulary places the work at the top of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The title âImpact of Different Acts of National Security on U.S. Airport Protocolsâ is precise and effectively frames the investigationâs scope. It immediately signals the historical focus and key legislation, aiding clarity and reader orientation.
The research question is stated clearly on the title page, guiding the entire essay. Its specificity regarding the ATSA, PATRIOT Act and JFK airport ensures focused historical inquiry. Well done.
Including the word count (3928) meets IB requirements and confirms adherence to the 4000-word limit. This demonstrates attention to administrative formatting criteria.
The table of contents lists all required sections and corresponding page ranges, showing a coherent structure and roadmap for the reader. This organization strengthens the essayâs navigability.
The repeated line âEssay Title: â¦ Research Question: â¦â duplicates information already given. Consider removing this to streamline the table of contents page and avoid redundancy.
Some table cell widths appear inconsistent or collapsed (e.g., cells 86.81â87.81). Verify the tableâs formatting to ensure each entry is legible and evenly spaced.
The heading âSecurity Protocols at John. F Kennedy International Airportâ clearly signals the shift to case study analysis. This strong signposting supports reader engagement.
The analysis of pre-9/11 prescreening tools is informative, but linking these directly to the objectives of the ATSA and PATRIOT Act would improve analytical depth.
Footnote markers such as â17 Ibidâ and â19 Ibidâ interrupt narrative flow. Minimize reliance on âIbid.â and incorporate full citations for clarity and consistency.
The description of CAPPS flags is clear, but adding quantitative data (e.g., percentage of flagged passengers) would better support claims of its ineffectiveness.
The blank page 3 is extraneous. Unless required by pageânumbering conventions, consider removing it to maintain flow.
The âIntroductionâ heading is clearly signposted, establishing a logical structure. This effective signposting meets presentation requirements for clear sectioning.
The narrative of the 9/11 events is descriptive but lacks historiographical context or scholarly debate. Integrate academic perspectives on causation to deepen analysis.
Footnote markers appear as HTML artifacts (â<sup>2</sup>â), suggesting formatting errors. Ensure superscripts are correctly rendered throughout.
The transition at the start of page 5 outlines the essayâs methodological approachâcomparing JFK with other airports and examining policy debates. This roadmap is useful and focused.
The paperâs outline paragraph clearly sets out the sequence of analysis (events, JFK protocols, comparisons, evaluation). Such structuring effectively maintains focus on the research question.
The thesis uses the verb âprove.â In historical analysis, replace this with âargueâ or âdemonstrateâ to reflect critical, evidence-based evaluation.
Repeating the research question verbatim here reinforces focus, but consider integrating it into the introduction narrative rather than isolating it as a block quote.
The descriptive overview of the World Trade Center and Pentagon is accurate but could be linked back to how security vulnerabilities at these sites informed airport protocols.
Footnotes labeled âlbidâ and âlhidâ are inconsistent and unclear. Standardize to âIbid.â with proper superscript formatting to improve citation clarity.
The description of Al Qaedaâs motives is general; incorporate a scholarly source analyzing ideology and strategy to deepen understanding of the groupâs threat to aviation.
The paragraph on ATSA and PATRIOT Act impacts is comprehensive but very dense. Break it into two or more paragraphs for readability.
Long in-text footnotes marked with numeric MathML (e.g., â<math>26</math> Ibidâ) should be converted to standard superscript numerals to maintain formatting consistency.
The âComparison with Security Protocols Followed at Other Major Airportsâ heading clearly marks a shift to broader analysis. This improves structural coherence.
Opening this section by linking back to the 9/11 focus and research question reinforces coherence. Excellent connection to prior material.
The designation of JFK as a heightened threat area is asserted, but adding evidence (e.g., threat-level metrics or official risk assessments) would strengthen critical evaluation.
